name: backend-api
description: Design and implement RESTful API endpoints following best practices for routing, versioning, and HTTP standards. Use this skill when creating or modifying API routes, controllers, or endpoints in files like routes/api.php, API controller files in app/Http/Controllers/Api/, controller files in app/Http/Controllers/, API resource classes, or when implementing REST API functionality. Use when working on URL structures, HTTP method handling (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), API versioning strategies, query parameter handling for filtering/sorting/pagination, HTTP status code responses, rate limiting configuration, or any API endpoint design decisions.
capabilities: Design RESTful API endpoints with proper HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE)
Implement API versioning strategies (URL path or header-based)
Configure query parameters for filtering, sorting, and pagination
Apply correct HTTP status codes for API responses
Set up rate limiting and API throttling configurations
Create API resource transformation classes for response formatting
author: DevanB
securitySummary: This is a pure documentation skill containing only markdown guidance and JSON metadata. No executable code, no network operations, no filesystem modifications. All 38 static findings are false positives triggered by benign text patterns in documentation. The skill only provides API design guidance and does not access, store, or transmit any data.
valueStatement: Building APIs requires careful attention to routing, versioning, and HTTP standards to ensure maintainability and usability. This skill provides expert guidance on creating clean, well-structured APIs that follow industry best practices.
userTitle: Design RESTful APIs with Best Practices
limitations: Provides guidance only; does not generate or modify code directly
Focuses on RESTful patterns; may not cover GraphQL or gRPC APIs
Assumes existing Laravel or similar backend framework knowledge

useCases.title: Build API Endpoints
useCases.description: Design and implement new RESTful API endpoints following industry standards and best practices.

useCases.title: Maintain Existing APIs
useCases.description: Extend or refactor existing API routes and controllers with consistent patterns.

useCases.title: Design API Strategy
useCases.description: Plan API versioning strategies and response formatting approaches for new projects.

promptTemplates.title: Create Resource Endpoint
promptTemplates.scenario: Building a new API endpoint for a resource
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a RESTful API endpoint for managing [resource_name]. Include proper routing, controller methods for CRUD operations, and appropriate HTTP status codes.

promptTemplates.title: Add Pagination
promptTemplates.prompt: Add pagination support to the existing [endpoint_name] API endpoint. Include query parameters for page number and limit, with proper response metadata.

promptTemplates.title: Version API
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement API versioning for [api_name]. Should I use URL path versioning (/api/v1/) or header-based versioning? Provide configuration and migration steps.

promptTemplates.title: Add Rate Limiting
promptTemplates.prompt: Add rate limiting to the [endpoint_name] API endpoint. Configure appropriate limits per user and include rate limit headers in responses.

bestPractices: Use consistent URL naming conventions (plural nouns for resources, kebab-case)
Always return appropriate HTTP status codes (200, 201, 400, 404, 422, 500)
Include pagination metadata and rate limit headers in all list endpoints
antiPatterns: Using verbs in URLs instead of nouns (e.g., /getUsers instead of /users)
Returning different response structures for success and error cases
Exposing internal database IDs in API responses; use UUIDs instead

faq.question: Which API versioning approach should I use?
faq.answer: URL path versioning (/api/v1/) is simpler and more discoverable. Header-based versioning is cleaner but requires more client setup.

faq.question: What HTTP status codes should I use?
faq.answer: Use 200 for success, 201 for created resources, 400 for bad requests, 401 for auth failures, 404 for not found, 422 for validation errors, and 500 for server errors.

faq.question: How do I handle pagination efficiently?
faq.answer: Use cursor-based pagination for large datasets or offset-based with limit parameters. Always include total count and next/previous links.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This is a prompt-based skill that only provides guidance. It does not access, store, or transmit any data.

faq.question: Why is my API slow with pagination?
faq.answer: Ensure proper database indexes on filtered columns. Use cursor pagination for large datasets to avoid OFFSET calculation overhead.

faq.question: How does this compare to API management platforms?
faq.answer: This skill provides coding guidance for building APIs. It does not replace API gateways or management platforms that handle authentication, caching, and monitoring.
